event: Eve in the Garden of Eden
symbols: bitten fruit in hand — the forbidden fruit of the tree of knowledge, the moment of disobedience that inaugurates the Fall
nakedness — the pre-Fall state of innocence (Genesis 2:25), yet also the vulnerability that follows transgression
small snake at her feet — the serpent of Genesis 3:1, the tempter who deceived Eve
scattered fruits on the ground — the abundance of Eden now associated with the fruit of transgression
voluptuous body — Eve as the mother of all living (Genesis 3:20), embodiment of fecundity and the fullness of created flesh
dark background — the spiritual darkness that enters through the Fall, the loss of Edenic light
leaf necklace — a vestige of the fig leaves used to cover nakedness after the eyes of both were opened (Genesis 3:7)
description: A full-length frontal portrait of a nude woman standing against a dark background. She has long blonde hair falling past her shoulders and holds a small green fruit with a bite taken from it in her right hand near her midsection. A thin golden snake coils at her feet among several small yellow-green fruits scattered on the ground. She wears a small leaf-shaped pendant on a chain around her neck. Her body is rendered with exaggerated rounded proportions, filling the tall vertical composition.
theological_description: This portrayal of Eve captures the pivotal moment of Genesis 3 — the mother of all living stands with the forbidden fruit already bitten, the act of disobedience accomplished. The snake at her feet recalls God's pronouncement of enmity between the serpent and the woman (Genesis 3:15), the protoevangelium that promises a future seed who will crush the serpent's head. Eve's nakedness carries a theological duality: Genesis 2:25 tells us the man and woman were naked and unashamed, yet after eating the fruit their eyes were opened and they knew they were naked (Genesis 3:7). The exaggerated physicality of the figure emphasizes Eve as flesh — the very flesh God formed and declared good, now caught in the tension between created goodness and fallen condition. Paul reads this event as the entry of sin and death into the world through one transgression (Romans 5:12), establishing the need for the second Adam. In patristic typology, Eve is contrasted with Mary: as Eve's disobedience brought the curse, Mary's obedient fiat (Luke 1:38) inaugurated salvation. The dark background suggests the cosmic shift from light to shadow that follows the Fall, the expulsion from paradise yet to come (Genesis 3:23-24).

scripture_refs.reason: Eve takes the forbidden fruit and eats — the central action depicted in the painting
The serpent's temptation of Eve, visible as the snake at her feet
They were both naked and not ashamed — Eve's nakedness before the full realization of shame
The protoevangelium — enmity between the serpent and the woman, the seed who will crush the serpent
Through one person sin entered the world — Paul's theology of the Fall rooted in Eve and Adam's transgression
Mary's fiat as the reversal of Eve's disobedience — patristic Eve-Mary typology
Adam named his wife Eve because she was the mother of all the living
